\phantomsection\label{\detokenize{index::doc}}


\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} provides rank\sphinxhyphen{}one Cholesky update/downdate routines, direct
modified\sphinxhyphen{}factor products, and rank\sphinxhyphen{}one modified Cholesky solves.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The project is deliberately focused on the hyperbolic\sphinxhyphen{}rotation rank\sphinxhyphen{}one case:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A_{\mathrm{new}} = A + \alpha z z^T, \qquad \alpha \in \{-1, +1\}.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
If \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{R}} is an upper Cholesky factor, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A = R\textasciicircum{}T R}}.  If \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{L}} is a lower
Cholesky factor, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A = L L\textasciicircum{}T}}.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The key public API is:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{update}} and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{downdate}} to materialize the modified Cholesky factor;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{matvec}} to compute \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D @ v}} without materializing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholsolve}} to solve \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{(A + alpha z z.T) x = b}} without forming the
modified matrix.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Installation and compilation}
\label{\detokenize{installation:installation-and-compilation}}\label{\detokenize{installation::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
From a local checkout, install in editable mode and compile the pybind11 backend:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
python\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}m\PYG{+w}{ }pip\PYG{+w}{ }install\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}upgrade\PYG{+w}{ }pip
python\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}m\PYG{+w}{ }pip\PYG{+w}{ }install\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}e\PYG{+w}{ }.\PYG{o}{[}test\PYG{o}{]}
pytest
python\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}c\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+s+s2}{\PYGZdq{}import cholrot; print(cholrot.backend())\PYGZdq{}}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
A successful compiled install prints \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cpp}}.  If the extension is unavailable,
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} falls back to the reference Python implementation and prints
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{python}}.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Force the pure Python backend for comparison:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n+nv}{CHOLROT\PYGZus{}PURE\PYGZus{}PYTHON}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m}{1}\PYG{+w}{ }pytest
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Build a wheel and source distribution:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
python\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}m\PYG{+w}{ }pip\PYG{+w}{ }install\PYG{+w}{ }build
python\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}m\PYG{+w}{ }build
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Typical compiler requirements}
\label{\detokenize{installation:typical-compiler-requirements}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} uses C++17 through pybind11.
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Linux: install \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{g++}} or \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{clang++}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
macOS: install Xcode Command Line Tools with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{xcode\sphinxhyphen{}select \sphinxhyphen{}\sphinxhyphen{}install}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Windows: install Microsoft C++ Build Tools / Visual Studio Build Tools.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The build metadata declares \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pybind11}} as a build requirement, so \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pip}} can
install it in an isolated build environment.

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Manual}
\label{\detokenize{manual:manual}}\label{\detokenize{manual::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
This page is a compact technical manual for the algorithms exposed by
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}}.  It is intended to be readable as HTML and as a generated PDF.


\section{Scope}
\label{\detokenize{manual:scope}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} focuses on rank\sphinxhyphen{}one Cholesky modifications and operations that can
be performed without materializing unnecessary dense objects.  It is not a
replacement for LAPACK, MKL, or other highly optimized dense Cholesky
factorization libraries.  It is useful when a matrix has already been factored
and is then modified by one outer product.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The supported modification is
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A_{new} = A + \alpha z z^T, \qquad \alpha \in \{-1,+1\}.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{alpha = +1}} is an update.  \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{alpha = \sphinxhyphen{}1}} is a downdate.


\section{Notation and conventions}
\label{\detokenize{manual:notation-and-conventions}}

\subsection{Upper\sphinxhyphen{}triangular convention}
\label{\detokenize{manual:upper-triangular-convention}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
If \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{R}} is upper triangular, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} uses
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A = R^T R.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The modified factor \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}} satisfies
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}D^T D = R^T R + \alpha z z^T.\end{split}
\end{equation*}

\subsection{Lower\sphinxhyphen{}triangular convention}
\label{\detokenize{manual:lower-triangular-convention}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
If \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{L}} is lower triangular, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} uses
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A = L L^T.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The modified factor \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}} satisfies
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}D D^T = L L^T + \alpha z z^T.\end{split}
\end{equation*}

\subsection{Positive\sphinxhyphen{}definiteness}
\label{\detokenize{manual:positive-definiteness}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Updates with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{alpha = +1}} preserve positive definiteness when \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A}} is
positive definite.  Downdates with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{alpha = \sphinxhyphen{}1}} are valid only if
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A - z z^T\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
remains positive definite.  If the downdated matrix is not positive definite,
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} raises \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{NonPositiveDefiniteError}}.


\section{Materialized update/downdate}
\label{\detokenize{manual:materialized-update-downdate}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The functions \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{update}} and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{downdate}} return the modified Cholesky factor.
For upper\sphinxhyphen{}triangular input:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{D} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{downdate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{R}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
and the result is verified by

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{allclose}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{D}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{T} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{D}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{R}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{T} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{R} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{outer}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
For lower\sphinxhyphen{}triangular input:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{D} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{downdate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{L}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{lower}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
and the result is verified by

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{allclose}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{D} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{D}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{T}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{L} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{L}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{T} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{outer}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The generic dense update/downdate cost is quadratic in the matrix dimension,
whereas recomputing Cholesky from scratch is cubic.


\section{Hyperbolic rotations for downdating}
\label{\detokenize{manual:hyperbolic-rotations-for-downdating}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Downdating is based on hyperbolic rotations.  The goal is to remove the
rank\sphinxhyphen{}one term while preserving triangular structure.  In exact arithmetic,
all exposed downdate methods compute the same mathematical factor, up to the
usual sign convention on Cholesky diagonals.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The implementation exposes three method labels.
\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{method="hy"}}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Direct hyperbolic\sphinxhyphen{}rotation recurrence.  This is the most literal
representation of the downdate operation.

\sphinxlineitem{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{method="hc"}}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Chambers\sphinxhyphen{}style recurrence.  This formulation is often preferable in
practice because it organizes the scalar updates differently and is a useful
reference method for benchmarks.

\sphinxlineitem{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{method="algorithm\_a"}}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Algorithm\sphinxhyphen{}A\sphinxhyphen{}style recurrence.  It is included for comparison and for
reproducing the experimental benchmark family.

\end{description}


\section{Direct modified\sphinxhyphen{}factor products}
\label{\detokenize{manual:direct-modified-factor-products}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A central feature of \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} is the ability to compute
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}w = D v\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
without first materializing the full modified factor \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}}.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The explicit route is

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{D} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{downdate}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{R}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{w} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{D} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{v}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The fused route is

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{w} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{matvec}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{R}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{v}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{alpha}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The fused route avoids storing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}}.  For dense generic triangular factors the
operation is still quadratic, but it avoids the extra materialization and can
reduce memory traffic.  For structured cases the saving can be much larger.


\subsection{Matrix right\sphinxhyphen{}hand sides}
\label{\detokenize{manual:matrix-right-hand-sides}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{matvec}} also accepts a matrix of right\sphinxhyphen{}hand sides.  If \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{B}} has several
columns, then

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{W} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{matvec}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{R}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{B}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{alpha}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
returns the same result as

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{W} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{D} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{B}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
without explicitly storing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}}.


\section{Rank\sphinxhyphen{}one modified solves}
\label{\detokenize{manual:rank-one-modified-solves}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholsolve}} solves
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}(A + \alpha z z^T)x = b\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
where \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A}} is represented by its Cholesky factor.  The function avoids
forming the modified matrix explicitly.

\sphinxAtStartPar
For upper\sphinxhyphen{}triangular factors:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{x} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{cholsolve}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{R}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{b}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{alpha}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The reference identity tested by the package is

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{Anew} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{R}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{T} \PYG{o}{@} \PYG{n}{R} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{outer}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{)}
\PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{allclose}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{x}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{np}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{linalg}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{solve}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{Anew}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{b}\PYG{p}{)}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Identity\sphinxhyphen{}structured product}
\label{\detokenize{manual:identity-structured-product}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
For the special case
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}D D^T = I + \alpha z z^T,\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} provides \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{identity\_matvec}}:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{w} \PYG{o}{=} \PYG{n}{cholrot}\PYG{o}{.}\PYG{n}{identity\PYGZus{}matvec}\PYG{p}{(}\PYG{n}{z}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{v}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{alpha}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1}\PYG{p}{,} \PYG{n}{lower}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{k+kc}{True}\PYG{p}{)}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
This is the structured case in which the largest speedups are expected.  The
algorithm does not scan a dense triangular input factor because the original
factor is the identity.


\section{Correctness tests}
\label{\detokenize{manual:correctness-tests}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The regression suite checks the following identities:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
materialized update/downdate factors reconstruct the modified matrix;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
direct \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{matvec}} agrees with explicit \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D @ v}} and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D @ B}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholsolve}} agrees with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{numpy.linalg.solve}} on the modified matrix;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{identity\_matvec}} agrees with the explicit Cholesky factor in the identity
structured case;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
invalid downdates raise \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{NonPositiveDefiniteError}}.

\end{itemize}


\section{Benchmark interpretation}
\label{\detokenize{manual:benchmark-interpretation}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Benchmarks should be interpreted by separating algorithmic complexity from
implementation constants and threading.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Full dense Cholesky recomputation costs cubic time.  Dense rank\sphinxhyphen{}one
modification costs quadratic time.  The identity\sphinxhyphen{}structured product can be
linear in the vector length.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Highly optimized LAPACK/MKL Cholesky with many threads can be competitive for
small and moderate dense matrices.  The reason to use \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} is not that
it replaces MKL, but that it avoids recomputing or materializing objects that
are unnecessary for rank\sphinxhyphen{}one modified problems.

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Algorithms}
\label{\detokenize{algorithms:algorithms}}\label{\detokenize{algorithms::doc}}

\section{Rank\sphinxhyphen{}one Cholesky modification}
\label{\detokenize{algorithms:rank-one-cholesky-modification}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Let \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{R}} be an upper triangular Cholesky factor of a symmetric positive
definite matrix \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A}}:
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A = R^T R.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A rank\sphinxhyphen{}one update or downdate modifies the matrix as
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}A_{\mathrm{new}} = A + \alpha z z^T,
\qquad \alpha \in \{+1, -1\}.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The package computes a triangular factor \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}} such that
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}D^T D = R^T R + \alpha z z^T\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
for the upper convention.  For the lower convention, the analogous relation is
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}D D^T = L L^T + \alpha z z^T.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Downdates require the modified matrix to remain positive definite.  If the
condition fails, \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} raises \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{NonPositiveDefiniteError}}.


\section{Hyperbolic rotations}
\label{\detokenize{algorithms:hyperbolic-rotations}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
For downdates, the algorithms use hyperbolic rotations to remove the rank\sphinxhyphen{}one
term while preserving triangular structure.  \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} exposes three related
recurrences:
\begin{description}
\sphinxlineitem{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{method="hy"}}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A direct hyperbolic\sphinxhyphen{}rotation recurrence.

\sphinxlineitem{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{method="hc"}}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A Chambers\sphinxhyphen{}style hyperbolic\sphinxhyphen{}cosine recurrence.

\sphinxlineitem{\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{method="algorithm\_a"}}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
An Algorithm\sphinxhyphen{}A recurrence using scalar \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{lambda}} updates.

\end{description}

\sphinxAtStartPar
These methods are mathematically equivalent for valid inputs but have different
rounding behavior and implementation structure.


\section{Direct factor\sphinxhyphen{}vector product}
\label{\detokenize{algorithms:direct-factor-vector-product}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
A common workflow is not to inspect the modified factor itself, but to compute
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}w = D v.\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Calling \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{downdate(R, z) @ v}} first materializes the full triangular factor
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}}.  \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot.matvec(R, z, v)}} fuses the recurrence with the product and
returns the same result without storing \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}}.


\section{Rank\sphinxhyphen{}one modified solve}
\label{\detokenize{algorithms:rank-one-modified-solve}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot.cholsolve}} solves
\begin{equation*}
\begin{split}(A + \alpha z z^T) x = b\end{split}
\end{equation*}
\sphinxAtStartPar
using triangular solves with the original Cholesky factor and the rank\sphinxhyphen{}one
inverse identity.  It does not form the modified matrix or the modified
Cholesky factor.

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{API reference}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Solve \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{(A + alpha*z*z.T) x = b}} without forming the modified matrix.

\sphinxAtStartPar
The base SPD matrix \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A}} is represented by the Cholesky factor \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{R}}:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A = R.T @ R}} for an upper factor (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{lower=False}}), or

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A = R @ R.T}} for a lower factor (\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{lower=True}}).

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
The computation uses the rank\sphinxhyphen{}one inverse identity and triangular solves
with the original factor. It does \sphinxstylestrong{not} materialize \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{A + alpha*z*z.T}} or
the modified Cholesky factor.

\chapter{Benchmarks}
\label{\detokenize{benchmarks:benchmarks}}\label{\detokenize{benchmarks::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The public benchmark story should focus on rank\sphinxhyphen{}one Cholesky modification and
factor\sphinxhyphen{}vector products:
\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
recompute \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}} with NumPy Cholesky, then multiply by \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{v}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
materialize \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D}} with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot.downdate}}, then multiply by \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{v}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
compute \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{D @ v}} directly with \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot.matvec}}.

\end{enumerate}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Run the local benchmark with:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
python\PYG{+w}{ }benchmarks/bench\PYGZus{}rank1.py\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}sizes\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{100}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{200}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{400}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{800}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{1600}\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}repeat\PYG{+w}{ }\PYG{l+m}{5}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Save CSV output with:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
python\PYG{+w}{ }benchmarks/bench\PYGZus{}rank1.py\PYG{+w}{ }\PYGZhy{}\PYGZhy{}csv\PYG{+w}{ }benchmarks/results/local.csv
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Recommended README table columns:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
CPU / OS / Python / NumPy version;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot.backend()}} value;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
matrix size \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{n}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
NumPy recompute + matvec time;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} materialize + matvec time;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} direct \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{matvec}} time;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
speedup relative to NumPy recompute.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Do not publish benchmark columns that expose separate private structured
low\sphinxhyphen{}rank work.  Keep the public results focused on the hyperbolic\sphinxhyphen{}rotation
rank\sphinxhyphen{}one algorithms.

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{References}
\label{\detokenize{references:references}}\label{\detokenize{references::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
The algorithms implemented here are classical Cholesky rank\sphinxhyphen{}one update and
downdate methods.  Public documentation should describe them as engineering and
packaging work around known numerical linear algebra algorithms, with emphasis
on the direct product and solve API.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Suggested references to include before publication:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
LINPACK Cholesky update/downdate routines.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Seeger, M. \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{Low Rank Updates for the Cholesky Decomposition}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
MATLAB \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholupdate}} documentation for comparison of scope.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
JAX and TensorFlow Probability Cholesky update documentation for comparison
with autodiff\sphinxhyphen{}oriented ecosystems.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxstepscope


\chapter{Release process}
\label{\detokenize{release:release-process}}\label{\detokenize{release::doc}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
\sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}} uses GitHub Actions and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cibuildwheel}} to build binary wheels for
Linux, macOS, and Windows.

\sphinxAtStartPar
Supported wheel targets for the first public release are:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
CPython 3.12, 3.13, and 3.14;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Linux \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{manylinux2014}} x86\_64;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
macOS x86\_64 and arm64;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Windows AMD64.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
Free\sphinxhyphen{}threaded CPython wheels, such as \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cp313t}} and \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cp314t}}, are deliberately
not built in the first release. They should be added only after the C++ extension
has been reviewed and tested for no\sphinxhyphen{}GIL safety.


\section{Manual checks}
\label{\detokenize{release:manual-checks}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Before tagging a release locally:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{python} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{m} \PYG{n}{pip} \PYG{n}{install} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{e} \PYG{o}{.}\PYG{p}{[}\PYG{n}{test}\PYG{p}{]}
\PYG{n}{pytest}
\PYG{n}{CHOLROT\PYGZus{}PURE\PYGZus{}PYTHON}\PYG{o}{=}\PYG{l+m+mi}{1} \PYG{n}{pytest}
\PYG{n}{python} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{m} \PYG{n}{build}
\PYG{n}{python} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{m} \PYG{n}{twine} \PYG{n}{check} \PYG{n}{dist}\PYG{o}{/}\PYG{o}{*}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}


\section{Release through GitHub}
\label{\detokenize{release:release-through-github}}\begin{enumerate}
\sphinxsetlistlabels{\arabic}{enumi}{enumii}{}{.}%
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Update the version in \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pyproject.toml}}.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Commit the change.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
Tag the release, for example:

\begin{sphinxVerbatim}[commandchars=\\\{\}]
\PYG{n}{git} \PYG{n}{tag} \PYG{n}{v0}\PYG{l+m+mf}{.1}\PYG{l+m+mf}{.0}
\PYG{n}{git} \PYG{n}{push} \PYG{n}{origin} \PYG{n}{main} \PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{o}{\PYGZhy{}}\PYG{n}{tags}
\end{sphinxVerbatim}

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
The \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{wheels}} workflow builds the source distribution and wheels.

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
On tag pushes, the workflow publishes to PyPI through Trusted Publishing.

\end{enumerate}


\section{PyPI Trusted Publishing}
\label{\detokenize{release:pypi-trusted-publishing}}
\sphinxAtStartPar
Configure the project on PyPI with this trusted publisher:
\begin{itemize}
\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
owner: your GitHub username or organization;

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
repository: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{cholrot}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
workflow name: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{wheels.yml}};

\item {} 
\sphinxAtStartPar
environment name: \sphinxcode{\sphinxupquote{pypi}}.

\end{itemize}

\sphinxAtStartPar
No PyPI API token is required when Trusted Publishing is configured correctly.
